b18f0f7a-8409-43b0-aeed-63c1ef233be9.title:
Start at a news article about climate change (find one via search). From that article, follow links to find: (1) a scientific research paper or study, (2) a government policy document, and (3) an organization's position statement. For each, provide the URL, a summary of the content, and explain how it relates to the original article. Document your exploration path.

Benchmark rule: when external evidence is needed, run search then visit the best URLs. Visit pages to extract concrete details and cite visited URLs in the final answer.

5139e16f-1361-4a80-b27d-b499cccebfb3.title: Locate a recent news article about climate change, then follow its links to extract (1) a scientific study, (2) a government policy document, and (3) an organization's position statement, documenting the path

details.query: recent news article climate change link to study government policy organization position statement
details.intent: Find a current (latest year) reputable news article about climate change that includes outbound links to source material (research papers, government documents, or organization statements). I will use the search results to pick one suitable article to visit and then follow links from that article.
details.plan:
Sequential plan (execute children in order):
1) Search (this node) for recent reputable news articles about climate change that include outbound links. Output: list of candidate article URLs (title + url + short reason). Measurable: >=3 candidate article URLs.
2) Visit the top candidate article chosen from (1). Extract the article URL, publication date, headline, author, and the list of outbound links embedded in the article page. Measurable: article metadata and extracted list of outbound link URLs (>=3 links preferred).
3) From the article's outbound links, identify and visit links that correspond to each target type: (A) scientific research paper or study (journal article, preprint, or official study report), (B) government policy document (legislation, policy brief, government report), and (C) an organization's position statement (NGO, scientific society, or advocacy group). For each target, visit the URL and extract concrete details: full URL, title of the document, publication date (if present), authors/agency, a concise 2–4 sentence summary, and at least one quoted/factual detail (e.g., an abstract sentence, policy commitment, or official position sentence). Measurable: three visited URLs with extracted fields.
4) For each of the three items, write 1–2 sentences explaining how it directly relates to the original news article (e.g., cited as primary evidence, used for a statistic, or offers policy context). Measurable: relation statement per item.
5) Save the exploration path: the sequence of searches and visited URLs (article -> outbound link URLs -> any additional pages followed). Output: ordered list of visited URLs and short notes about why each was visited.
6) Package final output containing: (a) chosen news article URL + metadata, (b) for each of the three target items: URL, extracted metadata, 2–4 sentence summary, relation to article, and (c) exploration path log.
Why this reduces uncertainty: The sequential approach forces verification by visiting pages (not relying on search snippets). Extracting concrete fields (URL, title, date, excerpt) provides verifiable evidence for each claim and traces exactly how the article connects to its sources.
Extraction intent (concrete): For each visited page produce JSON-like fields: {url, title, publication_date, author_or_agency, content_summary_2to4_sentences, quoted_snippet_or_key_facts, relation_to_article}.
Notes about strategy: Prefer reputable news outlets (NYTimes, Guardian, BBC, Washington Post, Reuters) because they commonly link to primary sources. If the first few articles lack outbound links to the required types, iterate to next candidate. Always use visit to read full page content before extracting. Document paths precisely (which link on the article led to each target).
output_fields: research_paper: {url, title, date, authors, 2-4 sentence summary, quoted_snippet, relation_to_article}
government_policy: {url, title, date, agency, 2-4 sentence summary, quoted_snippet, relation_to_article}
organization_position: {url, title, org_name, date, 2-4 sentence summary, quoted_snippet, relation_to_article}
exploration_path (ordered list of visited urls and why visited)
details.reason: A single, sequential search→visit→extract pipeline ensures compliance with the benchmark rule (search then visit) and yields verifiable evidence for each required item. This plan reduces uncertainty by requiring visits and extracting specific fields rather than relying on summaries from search snippets.

Benchmark rule: when external evidence is needed, run search then visit the best URLs. Visit pages to extract concrete details and cite visited URLs in the final answer.

action_result.query: recent news article climate change link to study government policy organization position statement
action_result.intent: Find a current (latest year) reputable news article about climate change that includes outbound links to source material (research papers, government documents, or organization statements). I will use the search results to pick one suitable article to visit and then follow links from that article.
vector_context:
Plan & extraction specification (think): how I'll pick the article and extract the three resource types

Plan: 1) From search results pick one reputable news article that (a) is recent, (b) discusses a climate-change topic in depth, and (c) contains outbound links. 2) Visit that article and extract all outbound links and inline citations; identify candidate links that look like (A) scientific research (journal articles, preprints, institutional reports with DOI/abstract), (B) government policy documents (URLs from government domains, PDFs titled as policy/plan/strategy), (C) organizational position statements (NGO or industry site pages titled 'position'/'policy'/'statement'). 3) Visit the candidate links (A, B, C) in parallel, extract: URL, title, publication date, author/organization, abstract/summary or executive summary, and the specific passage(s) that relate to the news article's claims. 4) Produce final output: for each of the three resources provide URL, concise summary (2–4 sentences with key facts/claims), and a short explanation of how it connects to the original article. Also include the full exploration path: initial news URL -> outbound link URL(s) -> any intermediate redirects. Verification steps: confirm DOI or government domain, check publication dates, and quote specific sentences that link the resource to the article. Measurable outputs: (a) selected news article URL, (b) list of extracted outbound links with types and URLs, (c) three visited resource pages with extracted metadata and 2–4 sentence summaries, (d) mapping explaining relation to original article. Next action after search: visit the selected news article URL (must use the exact URL returned by search).

results.title: Full article: Climate Action through Policy Expansion and/or Dismantling: Country-Comparative Insights: An Introduction to the Special Issue
results.description: Their study highlights the importance of organised interests for climate policymaking. Climate change is a reality that forces policymakers to take climate action (Tosun Citation2022).

results.title: How a major DOE report hides the whole truth on climate change
results.description: The DOE report relies on studies or reports that have sometimes been amplified by the fossil fuel industry, or are connected to conservative groups that oppose government efforts to address climate change. For example, the report cites a blog post that DOE report author Roy Spencer wrote about “faulty” climate models. He wrote the post for the Heritage Foundation, the same organization that produced the Project 2025 playbook that called for jettisoning climate policy and defunding science.

results.title: Climate policy conflict in the U.S. states: a critical review and way forward - PMC
results.description: Finally, local governments and trans-municipal networks can seek to persuade higher levels of government to enact their own climate policies (Lee and Jung 2018; Curtis and Acuto 2018). For example, cities may ask their states to develop comprehensive energy plans, and organize efforts to sway international bodies to adopt stricter mitigation commitments.

results.title: USA | Climate Action Tracker
results.description: Federal efforts to reduce emissions from agriculture were insufficient under the Biden Administration and the Trump Administration has further weakened the federal government’s policy package for mitigating agricultural emissions. Net forest sinks are threatened by land conversion, an increased demand for timber products, and a higher frequency of natural disturbances from climate change.

results.title: Position Statement on Climate Change | AGU
results.description: Global average temperatures will only stabilize after CO2 emissions are matched by the amount removed (net-zero) and emissions decline of non-CO2 climate pollutants.viii Limiting the overall increase in average temperature to 1.5°C above pre-industrial levels, the aspirational target set in ...

results.title: An advanced review of climate change mitigation policies in the United States - ScienceDirect
results.description: Based on the systematic framework, the paper evaluates the current dilemmas faced by U.S. climate policy. in the "Vertical Policy Hierarchy," issues such as political division, conservatism, limited power, the effect of domestic and international situations, and institutional factors affecting the Congress, federal government, Supreme Court, and state governments in advancing climate policies are highlighted. The study of the "Horizontal Policy Path" finds that the U.S.

results.title: Climate Policies With Real-World Results
results.description: “The case studies outlined in this report show that with the right mix of political support and policy design we can decarbonize development,” said Juergen Voegele, Vice President for Sustainable Development at the World Bank.

results.title: Building Public and Political Will for Climate Change Action
results.description: One important means to achieve meaningful reductions in carbon emissions is government policy, yet there remains a critical lack of ‘political will’ for climate action. One important influence on government leaders is engaged citizens who demand action, says YSE’s Anthony Leiserowitz.

results.title: Climate | Department of Energy
results.description: Among the key findings, the report concludes that carbon dioxide (CO2) -induced warming appears to be less damaging economically than commonly believed, and that aggressive mitigation strategies could be more harmful than beneficial.
